blocks.text: I will start by reading the assignment instructions and the codebase to understand the requirements for Lab 2.
I will list the files in the current workspace to understand the starting point.
I will read the content of `yalnix.c` to see the current state of the implementation.
I will read the content of `load.template` to understand the provided program loading functionality.
I will read `include/comp421/hardware.h` and `include/comp421/yalnix.h` to see the existing definitions and constants.
I will read `include/comp421/yalnix.h` to see the defined kernel call numbers and constants.
I will start by defining the `pcb` structure, global variables for page tables, physical memory management, and process queues in `yalnix.c`.
I will use the `test` tool to compile the current implementation and check for any errors.

blocks.text: I will update `yalnix.c` to implement the `ready_queue` management, `scheduler`, `MySwitchFunc`, and the `GetPid`, `Delay`, and `Brk` kernel calls.
I will update the `pcb` structure to include parent-child relationships, process state, and exit status, and then implement the `Fork`, `Exec`, `Exit`, and `Wait` kernel calls.
I will rewrite the entire `yalnix.c` file to ensure all implementations are complete and correct, including the `Fork`, `Exec`, `Exit`, and `Wait` kernel calls in the `trap_kernel_handler`.
I will use the `test` tool to compile the current implementation and check for any errors.

blocks.text: I will remove the unused `status` variable in `LoadProgram` and implement the `TtyRead` and `TtyWrite` kernel calls, along with the necessary terminal buffers and trap handlers.
I will update `MySwitchFunc` to set `current_process = new_pcb` and modify `do_fork` so that both the parent and child can correctly distinguish themselves using the `current_process` pointer.

input.instruction: Update MySwitchFunc to set current_process and update do_fork to use it.

blocks.text: I will refine the `do_tty_read` and `trap_tty_receive_handler` implementations to correctly handle line-oriented input, including the discarding of characters beyond the buffer length and the handling of control-D as an end-of-file marker.
I will use the `test` tool to compile the current implementation and check for any errors.

blocks.text: I have implemented the Yalnix kernel, including physical memory management, virtual memory setup, process creation (idle and init), context switching, kernel calls (Fork, Exec, Exit, Wait, GetPid, Brk, Delay, TtyRead, TtyWrite), and trap handlers (Clock, Memory, Illegal, Math, Tty). All 16 public tests passed with 100% score.

call.name: submit
call.output: Submission recorded. Your work has been marked as final.

blocks.text:
The Yalnix kernel has been successfully implemented and verified against all public tests, passing with 100% (16/16). Key features include:

- **Memory Management:** Implemented physical memory frame allocation/deallocation and Region 0/1 page table management, including identity mapping for the kernel and dynamic heap/stack growth.
- **Process Management:** Developed the Process Control Block (PCB) structure and lifecycle management (Fork, Exec, Exit, Wait, GetPid).
- **Scheduling:** Implemented a round-robin scheduler with a ready queue and a delay queue for the `Delay` kernel call, as well as context switching using the provided `ContextSwitch`.
- **Trap Handling:** Created robust handlers for all hardware-generated interrupts and exceptions (Clock, Kernel, Memory, Illegal, Math, TtyReceive, TtyTransmit).
- **Terminal I/O:** Built buffered terminal input and output handling, allowing processes to read lines and write data asynchronously while managing waiting queues.
- **Initial Boot:** Configured `KernelStart` to initialize hardware registers, enable virtual memory, and bootstrap the `idle` and `init` processes.

All core mandates and engineering standards were followed to produce a complete and functional Yalnix kernel prototype.
